walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's place the main game board in the center of the table. This is a fixed map of hexagonal terrain spaces in seven different types: Plains, Swamp, Lakes, Forest, Mountains, Wasteland, and Desert. Every game uses the same map, so learning it over time is rewarding.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Cult Board
walkthrough.text: Place the cult board nearby where everyone can see it. It has four cult tracks: Fire, Water, Earth, and Air. These tracks are a major source of endgame scoring. Each player will advance on these tracks by sending priests and using favor tiles.

walkthrough.title: Place Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the six scoring tiles and place one face up in each of the six round slots on the board. These tiles determine which actions give bonus victory points each round. Read all six now so you can plan your entire game around them.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place all bonus tiles face up near the board. The number of bonus tiles equals the number of players plus three. These tiles provide income and special actions, and you will draft a new one each time you pass. Having extra tiles ensures everyone has meaningful choices.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Shared Components
walkthrough.text: Place favor tiles, town tiles, and action tokens within reach of all players. Set out the general supply of workers, coins, and priests. Place bridge pieces where everyone can access them. These shared components are used throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Each player selects a faction. There are fourteen factions, two per terrain type. Each faction has unique starting resources, abilities, and building costs. For your first game, choose a faction recommended for beginners like the Witches or Nomads.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Faction Board
walkthrough.text: Take your faction board and place all wooden structures on their designated building track slots. Structures cover resource income values on the tracks. As you build and place structures on the map, you uncover higher income. This is the engine-building core of the game.

walkthrough.title: Configure Your Power Bowls
walkthrough.text: Set up your three power bowls on the faction board with the starting power tokens as indicated. Power cycles from Bowl one to Bowl two to Bowl three. Only power in Bowl three can be spent. Managing this cycle efficiently is one of the game's key skills.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Take your starting workers, coins, and priests as shown on your faction board. Each faction starts with different amounts, so follow your board exactly. These initial resources fund your first few turns, so managing them wisely sets the tone for your game.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Dwellings
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player places two starting dwellings on the map on hexes of their native terrain type. This is a critical decision because adjacency determines where you can expand. Place near opponents to leech power, but leave room to grow.

walkthrough.title: Draft Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player selects their first bonus tile. The later you are in turn order, the earlier you pick your bonus tile, balancing out the advantage. Choose a tile that complements your faction's strengths and the first round's scoring tile.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on round one. Terra Mystica lasts exactly six rounds. Each round follows three phases: Income, Actions, and Cleanup. Let's set the marker and begin our quest to terraform and dominate.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's understand what Terra Mystica is about. We are competing factions building settlements on a shared map. Each faction can only build on its native terrain, so we must terraform other hexes before building. Over six rounds, we expand territory, upgrade buildings, advance on cult tracks, and form towns.

walkthrough.title: No Luck, Pure Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's appreciate something unique. Terra Mystica has no dice, no random card draws during play. Everything is deterministic. The only variability comes from faction selection, scoring tile order, and player decisions. This means every outcome is the result of your planning.

walkthrough.title: Income Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, everyone simultaneously collects income. Workers come from dwelling slots uncovered on your faction board. Coins come from trading houses. Priests come from temples and the sanctuary. Power comes from certain structures and favor tiles. Your bonus tile may also provide income.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: After income, we take turns performing one action each, going clockwise. After your action, play passes to the next player. This continues until everyone has passed. You can take as many total actions as you can afford before passing.

walkthrough.title: Transform and Build
walkthrough.text: The core action is terraforming a hex adjacent to your structures and then building a dwelling on it. Terraforming cost depends on how many steps your native terrain is from the target terrain on the transformation wheel. Each step costs workers. After terraforming to your terrain, build a dwelling for workers and coins.

walkthrough.title: The Transformation Wheel
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the wheel. The seven terrain types are arranged in a circle: Plains, Swamp, Lakes, Forest, Mountains, Wasteland, Desert, and back to Plains. Count the minimum steps between your native terrain and the target. Each step typically costs three workers, though some factions reduce this.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Structures
walkthrough.text: You can upgrade existing structures on the map. Dwellings upgrade to trading houses for coin income. Trading houses upgrade to either a temple for priest income and a favor tile, or a stronghold for your faction's unique ability. Temples upgrade to the sanctuary for another favor tile and priest income.

walkthrough.title: Send Priests to Cult Tracks
walkthrough.text: Place a priest on one of the four cult tracks to advance your position. Higher positions yield victory points at game end and during certain scoring rounds. Each cult track has limited priest slots, so timing matters. Reaching position ten first grants a special bonus.

walkthrough.title: Power Actions
walkthrough.text: Spend power from Bowl three to perform special actions. You can gain workers, coins, or priests, terraform one step for free, build a bridge, or gain seven victory points directly. Each power action space can only be used once per round across all players, so acting first is an advantage.

walkthrough.title: Special Actions and Passing
walkthrough.text: Some bonus tiles, favor tiles, and faction abilities grant special actions usable once per round. When you have no more actions to take or want to stop, you pass. Return your current bonus tile and draft a new one. Early passers get first pick of bonus tiles.

walkthrough.title: The Power Cycle
walkthrough.text: Power tokens move through three bowls. When you gain power, tokens move from Bowl one to Bowl two, then from Bowl two to Bowl three. Only Bowl three power can be spent. Spent power returns to Bowl one. Managing this cycle so power reaches Bowl three when you need it is a critical skill.

walkthrough.title: Leeching Power
walkthrough.text: When an opponent builds or upgrades adjacent to your structure, you may leech power equal to the structure's value. Dwellings give one, trading houses give two, temples and strongholds give three. But leeching costs one victory point per power gained minus one. This trade-off is one of the game's most important decisions.

walkthrough.title: Forming Towns
walkthrough.text: When you have a connected cluster of structures with a total power value of seven or more, you form a town. Take a town tile granting a one-time bonus such as victory points, resources, or cult advancement. Towns are key scoring milestones and the town tiles are limited.

walkthrough.title: Favor Tiles
walkthrough.text: You gain a favor tile each time you build a temple or the sanctuary. Each favor tile advances you on a cult track and provides an ongoing bonus like extra income or victory points per specific action. Favor tiles are limited, so choosing the right ones is strategic.

walkthrough.title: Bridges
walkthrough.text: Bridges connect non-adjacent hexes across rivers. Build them via power actions or special abilities. Bridged hexes count as adjacent for town formation and expansion purposes. Some factions like the Engineers have reduced bridge costs, making this a core part of their strategy.

walkthrough.title: Shipping
walkthrough.text: Some factions have a shipping value that extends adjacency across water hexes. Increasing your shipping value lets you treat distant hexes separated by water as adjacent. This expands your building options and helps connect structures for town formation.

walkthrough.title: Adjacency and Interaction
walkthrough.text: Adjacency is the heart of Terra Mystica. You can only terraform and build adjacent to existing structures. Building near opponents gives them power leeching opportunities but also lets you leech from them. Power actions and cult track positions are first-come-first-served, adding constant competition.

walkthrough.title: Round End
walkthrough.text: After all players pass, resolve the current round's scoring tile. For example, if it says two victory points per dwelling built this round, score accordingly. Then advance the round marker. After round six, proceed to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After six rounds, three final scoring categories are evaluated. First, in-game victory points accumulated throughout play. Second, cult track positions: eight victory points for first, four for second, two for third on each of the four tracks. Third, largest connected network: eighteen for first, twelve for second, six for third.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Add up all victory points from in-game scoring, cult tracks, and the network bonus. The player with the most total victory points wins. Ties are broken by most remaining resources. Every point matters in Terra Mystica, so efficient play throughout all six rounds is essential.

Terraform → build → expand → form towns over 6 rounds

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Terra Mystica
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Terra Mystica! We will walk through a practice first round with four players. Each player has chosen a faction, placed their two starting dwellings, and drafted a bonus tile. The scoring tiles are revealed and round one is about to begin.

walkthrough.title: Income Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's collect income. Everyone checks their faction board simultaneously. With two starting dwellings placed, each player has uncovered some worker income. Players also collect coins, priests, and power based on their faction's starting state. Do not forget your bonus tile income!

walkthrough.title: Check the Round Scoring Tile
walkthrough.text: Before taking actions, let's read the round one scoring tile. It says two victory points for each dwelling built this round. This means our priority should be building dwellings. Players who terraform and build dwellings will earn bonus VP on top of their expansion.

walkthrough.title: Player One Terraforms
walkthrough.text: Player one, playing the Witches on Forest terrain, looks at an adjacent Swamp hex. Forest is one step from Swamp on the transformation wheel, costing three workers. They spend the workers to terraform it to Forest, then build a dwelling for two workers and two coins. Two VP from the scoring tile!

walkthrough.title: Power Leeching Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Player one's new dwelling is adjacent to Player two's structure. Player two is offered the chance to leech power. The dwelling is worth one power. Leeching one power costs zero VP because the cost is power gained minus one. Player two accepts. Free power!

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Ignoring the Scoring Tile
walkthrough.text: Player three decides to upgrade a dwelling to a trading house instead of building new dwellings. The upgrade is useful long-term, but this round rewards dwelling builds. Player three misses out on easy VP while others score two points per dwelling built.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Align Actions with Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Always check what the current scoring tile rewards before choosing your action. If this round gives VP for dwellings, build dwellings. If next round rewards trading houses, plan your upgrades for then. Aligning your actions with the scoring tiles is one of the most important habits in Terra Mystica.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Takes a Power Action
walkthrough.text: Player two has cycled enough power to Bowl three and uses the power action to terraform one hex for free. Then they spend workers and coins to build a dwelling on it. This is a strong combo: free terraform plus paid dwelling equals efficient expansion.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Sends a Priest
walkthrough.text: Player four sends a priest to the Fire cult track, advancing two spaces. They chose Fire because a favor tile they want requires Fire advancement, and reaching certain positions early locks out opponents from the limited high slots.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Spreading Across All Cults
walkthrough.text: Player three sends priests to all four cult tracks, advancing one space on each. This spreads their presence thin. At game end, only the top three positions score significant points. Being fourth on all four tracks scores nothing. Focus beats breadth on cult tracks.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Focus on Two or Three Cult Tracks
walkthrough.text: Cult track scoring rewards the top positions disproportionately. Eight VP for first, four for second, two for third, and nothing below that. It is far better to place first on two tracks than to place fourth on all four. Concentrate your priests and favor tiles on two or three tracks maximum.

walkthrough.title: Leeching Decision
walkthrough.text: Player four builds adjacent to Player one. Player one is offered three power from the adjacent trading house. That would cost two VP. Player one considers: the power is valuable for next turn's actions, but two VP is steep. They accept because the power will fund a key terraform action next turn.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Bonus Tile Draft
walkthrough.text: Player three runs out of resources and passes first. They return their bonus tile and draft a new one for round two. Because they passed first, they get first pick of the remaining tiles. This is the trade-off: fewer actions this round but better tile selection for next round.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Hoarding Resources
walkthrough.text: Player four has leftover workers and coins at the end of the round but passed without using them. Unspent resources at game end are worth almost nothing. Every resource should be invested in building, upgrading, or converting to power actions. Do not sit on a pile of unused workers.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Everything
walkthrough.text: Terra Mystica rewards full investment. Workers, coins, and power that are not spent are essentially wasted. Before passing, look for any action you can take that advances your position, even if it is small. The difference between good and great players is resource efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Round One Scoring
walkthrough.text: All players have passed. Let's resolve the scoring tile. Player one built two dwellings this round for four VP. Player two built one dwelling for two VP. Player three built none and gets zero bonus VP. Player four built one for two VP. The scoring tile rewards aligned play.

walkthrough.title: Looking Ahead
walkthrough.text: Before round two begins, let's check the next scoring tile. It rewards trading house upgrades. Now Player three's earlier upgrade makes more sense for round two, but they could have timed it better. Reading ahead two to three rounds lets you sequence your plans optimally.

walkthrough.title: Town Planning Preview
walkthrough.text: Player one notices their three structures are close to forming a cluster with power value seven. One more dwelling or an upgrade could create a town. Town tiles provide powerful one-time bonuses and are limited. Racing to form towns is one of the game's most rewarding strategies.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first round of Terra Mystica! You learned about terraforming, power leeching, scoring tile alignment, cult track focus, and resource efficiency. These fundamentals will serve you well across all six rounds.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Terraform
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for faction-specific advice and town formation strategies.

Second, reference the Appendix for a complete glossary of every mechanism and faction ability.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Plan Starting Dwellings Carefully
walkthrough.text: Your two starting dwellings determine your entire game trajectory. Place where you have room to expand toward clusters for town formation. Place near opponents to leech power but not so close that you compete for the same hexes. Study the map and think three rounds ahead.

walkthrough.title: Build Near Opponents Deliberately
walkthrough.text: Building adjacent to opponents gives them leeching opportunities, but it also gives you the same. In most cases, the power gained from leeching is worth more than the VP cost. Do not isolate yourself on the map trying to avoid opponents.

walkthrough.title: Align with Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Read all six scoring tiles during setup and build a rough six-round plan. If round three rewards trading houses, plan your upgrades for round three. Scoring tile alignment can easily add twenty or more VP over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Form Towns Early
walkthrough.text: Town tiles are limited and powerful. Plan your building clusters to reach seven power value as quickly as possible. Two dwellings plus a trading house plus a temple equals seven. Forming towns in rounds two or three lets you use the tile bonus for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Focus Your Cult Tracks
walkthrough.text: Spreading across all four cult tracks is a losing strategy. Pick two or three tracks that align with your faction and favor tiles, and push hard for the top positions. Eight VP for first place on one track is worth more than two VP for third on four tracks.

walkthrough.title: Invest Everything
walkthrough.text: Unspent resources at game end are worth almost nothing. Before passing each round, look for any action that advances your position, even a small one. Build that extra dwelling, send a priest, or use a power action. Full investment is what separates competitive players.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Network
walkthrough.text: The largest connected network bonus is worth up to eighteen VP. From your very first dwelling placement, think about how you will connect your structures into one contiguous group. Bridges and shipping help span gaps. Do not split your network unless absolutely necessary.

walkthrough.title: Learn Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Each of the fourteen factions plays very differently. Your stronghold power defines your strategy. Study what makes your faction unique and build your entire game plan around it. Playing a generic strategy on a specialized faction will always underperform.

Think 3 rounds ahead from starting positions

bullets: Leeching power usually worth more than VP cost
Don't isolate yourself to avoid opponents

summary.title: Align with Scoring Tiles
bullets: Read all 6 tiles at setup → plan 6-round sequence
Alignment can add 20+ VP over the game

summary.title: Form Towns Early
bullets: 7 power value cluster = town (limited tiles)
2 dwellings + TH + temple = 7; aim for rounds 2-3

summary.title: Focus Your Cult Tracks
bullets: Pick 2-3 tracks; push for top positions
1st on 1 track (8 VP) > 3rd on 4 tracks (8 VP total)

summary.title: Invest Everything
bullets: Unspent resources ≈ wasted points
Always find 1 more action before passing

summary.title: Plan Your Network
bullets: Largest connected network = up to 18 VP
Never split network unless absolutely necessary

summary.title: Learn Your Faction
bullets: 14 factions play very differently
Build strategy around your stronghold power

walkthrough.title: Round Scoring Alignment Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their entire six-round sequence around the scoring tiles before taking a single action. If round one rewards dwellings and round two rewards trading houses, spend round one building dwellings and round two upgrading them. Every action should earn double value by aligning with the current scoring tile.

walkthrough.title: Town Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Forming your first town by round two gives you the best town tile and an early bonus that compounds throughout the game. Plan your dwelling placements and upgrades to reach seven power value in a connected cluster as quickly as possible. Some factions can form a town in round one with the right opening.

walkthrough.title: Power Cycle Optimization
walkthrough.text: The difference between a good and great player often comes down to power management. Accept leeching opportunities aggressively in rounds one and two when the VP cost is low relative to the power gained. Use power actions before opponents claim them. Burn power from Bowl one strategically when you need to accelerate the cycle.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Passing Order
walkthrough.text: Passing early lets you draft the best bonus tile, while passing late lets you take more actions. In rounds where a critical bonus tile is available, consider passing early to secure it even at the cost of one fewer action. Conversely, in rounds where actions are high-value, stay in as long as possible.

walkthrough.title: Cult Track Racing
walkthrough.text: Reaching position ten on a cult track before anyone else grants a one-time bonus that is often worth a town tile. If you have the priests and favor tiles to make a cult rush viable, commit fully. Reaching ten on even one track is a significant swing. Combine this with favor tiles that advance your position for free.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Denial
walkthrough.text: In tight games, denying opponents key hexes, power actions, or cult track positions can be worth as much as your own scoring. If an opponent needs one specific hex to form a town, terraform it first. If they need the seven VP power action, take it before them. Strategic denial separates intermediate from advanced play.

walkthrough.title: Faction-Specific Optimization
walkthrough.text: Each faction has an optimal opening and mid-game trajectory. Witches should rush a third dwelling for a free town. Darklings should convert priests into workers for aggressive terraforming. Engineers should build bridges to connect distant settlements. Learn your faction's unique cadence and execute it precisely.

walkthrough.title: Network Geometry
walkthrough.text: Plan your network expansion to maximize connected structures while minimizing the number of hexes you need to terraform. Build in lines rather than clusters when possible, because lines connect more territory with fewer gaps. Use bridges and shipping to span rivers without wasting actions on water-adjacent hexes.

summary.title: Round Scoring Alignment Mastery
bullets: Plan all 6 rounds around scoring tiles before acting
Every action should earn double value via alignment

summary.title: Town Rush Strategy
bullets: Town by round 2 = best tile + compounding bonus
Some factions can town in round 1

summary.title: Power Cycle Optimization
bullets: Leech aggressively in rounds 1-2
Use power actions before opponents claim them

summary.title: Tempo and Passing Order
bullets: Pass early for critical bonus tile
Stay late when actions are high-value

summary.title: Cult Track Racing
bullets: Position 10 first = town-tile-level bonus
Commit fully with priests + favor tiles

summary.title: Opponent Denial
bullets: Deny key hexes, power actions, cult positions
Strategic denial ≈ scoring for yourself

summary.title: Faction-Specific Optimization
bullets: Each faction has optimal opening + trajectory
Witches: rush town; Darklings: convert priests; Engineers: bridges

summary.title: Network Geometry
bullets: Lines > clusters for network efficiency
Use bridges/shipping to span rivers cheaply

entries.definition: The basic structure. Costs workers and coins to build on your native terrain. Uncovering dwelling slots on your faction board increases worker income. Power value: one.
Upgraded from a dwelling. Provides coin income. Costs workers and coins. Power value: two. Must be built adjacent to an opponent's structure in most cases.
Upgraded from a trading house. Provides priest income and grants a favor tile. Costs workers and coins. Power value: three.
Upgraded from a trading house. Unlocks your faction's unique special ability. Costs workers and coins. Power value: three. Can only build one.
Upgraded from a temple. Provides priest income and grants a second favor tile. Costs workers and coins. Power value: three. Can only build one.
Converting a hex from one terrain type to another. Cost is determined by the number of steps on the transformation wheel between the target and your native terrain.
A circular arrangement of seven terrain types. Count minimum steps from target to native terrain to determine terraforming cost. Each step costs workers.
The first bowl in the power cycle. Power starts here after being spent. When gaining power, tokens move from Bowl I to Bowl II first.
The middle bowl. Tokens move here from Bowl I when gaining power. Once all Bowl I tokens are in Bowl II, further power gains move tokens to Bowl III.
The spendable bowl. Only power in Bowl III can be used for power actions. Spent power returns to Bowl I.
When an opponent builds or upgrades adjacent to your structure, you may gain power equal to the adjacent structure's power value. Costs one VP per power gained minus one.
A connected cluster of structures with total power value of seven or more. Forming a town grants a town tile with one-time bonuses.
A reward tile gained when forming a town. Provides VP, resources, or cult advancement. Town tiles are limited and vary in power.
Gained from building temples and the sanctuary. Provides cult track advancement and an ongoing bonus such as income or VP per action.
One per round, determines which actions earn bonus VP that round. Read all six at setup to plan your game.
Drafted when passing. Provides income and sometimes a special action. Number of tiles equals players plus three.
Four tracks (Fire, Water, Earth, Air) on the cult board. Advance by sending priests or using favor tiles. Endgame scoring: eight/four/two VP for first/second/third.
Endgame scoring category. The player with the most structures in a single connected group scores eighteen VP. Second place: twelve. Third: six.
Connects two non-adjacent hexes across a river. Built via power action or special ability. Bridged hexes count as adjacent for towns and expansion.
A faction attribute that extends adjacency across water hexes. Higher shipping values let you treat more distant hexes as adjacent.
When you pass, return your bonus tile and draft a new one. You take no more actions this round. Early passers get first pick of tiles.
Plains faction. Gain extra VP for terraforming. Excellent at aggressive expansion across the map.
Plains faction. Gain cult advancement when opponents leech power from them. Strong cult track synergy.
Swamp faction. Can convert coins to VP or VP to coins at will. Economic flexibility and adaptability.
Swamp faction. Convert priests into workers for terraforming. Priest-hungry but extremely efficient builders.
Lakes faction. Can skip water hexes for town adjacency. Town-focused strategy with unique connectivity.
Lakes faction. Get discounts on many actions. Quantity-focused with lots of structures and buildings.
Forest faction. Place a free dwelling on Forest when founding a town. Fast expansion and easy town formation.
Forest faction. Gain favor tiles more easily. Strong cult track presence through extra favor tile acquisition.
Mountains faction. Can tunnel to terraform non-adjacent hexes. Flexible placement options that bypass normal adjacency.
Mountains faction. Reduced bridge costs and bridges count for town connections. Unique connectivity strategy.
Wasteland faction. Start with three dwellings instead of two. Strong early economy and board presence.
Wasteland faction. Only one starting dwelling but extremely powerful stronghold ability with double-action turns.
Desert faction. Can use priests to fly to non-adjacent hexes for building. Flexible but priest-intensive placement.
Desert faction. Terraform two hexes at once with their stronghold power. Efficient large-scale terrain conversion.
Each structure has a power value: Dwelling = 1, Trading House = 2, Temple = 3, Stronghold = 3, Sanctuary = 3. Used for leeching and town formation.
The first phase of each round. All players simultaneously collect workers, coins, priests, and power based on their faction board and bonus tile.
The main phase where players take turns performing one action each until all pass. Actions include terraform, build, upgrade, send priest, power action, special action, or pass.
One of six rounds in the game. Each round: Income Phase → Action Phase → Scoring Tile Resolution. Game ends after round six.
Hexes sharing an edge are adjacent. Most actions require adjacency to existing structures. Modified by bridges and shipping.
If players tie on total VP, the player with the most remaining resources (workers plus coins plus power in Bowl III) wins.
